title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — June 13, 2026
headline: The June 13 cycle is the benchmark's first documented government-mandated AI model shutdown: the US Commerce Department suspended Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all foreign nationals, and Anthropic complied transparently the same day — a conduct pattern that confirms, not lowers, its 59.1 score — while Bolivia's governance breakdown accumulates a further -4.1 delta just under the proposal threshold, and three absolute-floor confirmations carry fresh UN and HRW evidence of ongoing mass harm in Gaza, Sudan, and Russia.
summary: Twenty entities assessed, zero change proposals generated. This is a confirmation-dominant cycle with substantive new evidence across five analytical threads. The lead event is Anthropic's June 12 Fable 5/Mythos 5 export-control shutdown — the first government-mandated suspension of commercial AI models documented by the benchmark. The Commerce Department directive cited national security and a reported jailbreak; Anthropic disabled both models the same evening, issued a public apology, and disclosed the government's stated reasoning. The conduct pattern (same-day compliance, transparent disclosure, stated intent to restore access) is scored as mildly positive on the Accountability dimension and confirms, not lowers, the published 59.1 Functional score. A second analytical thread is Bolivia's continued deterioration: a research composite of 8.7 against a published 12.8 (delta -4.1) reflects the doubling of road blockades to ~100, dual ministerial resignations, and medicine-fuel-food shortages across La Paz and El Alto — with the state refusing to rule out military deployment. The delta sits 0.9 points below the 5-point proposal threshold; a state-of-emergency declaration would cross it. The US records a -2.8 research delta (14.7 vs. published 17.5) on fresh OBBBA Medicaid work-requirement evidence projecting ~7.8M coverage losses and ICE detention deaths accumulating at roughly one every six days with warehouse conversion underway. Three absolute-floor entities — Israel, Russia, Sudan — absorb new evidence from OCHA, HRW, and IOM respectively. The DRC Ebola PHEIC reaches 676 cases and 136 deaths by June 12, with the outbreak accelerating into new health zones ahead of the July peak window. Five Sahel countries confirm via freshness ruling: the FAO 52.8M lean-season projection used by the scanner was published January 27, 2026 — outside the 14-day window — and does not generate proposals this cycle.

topSignals.title: Anthropic 59.1 (Confirmed): First Government-Mandated AI Model Shutdown — Export-Control Compliance Scored as Transparent Conduct
topSignals.description: On June 12, 2026, the US Commerce Department issued an export-control directive requiring Anthropic to suspend its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all foreign nationals, including foreign-national Anthropic staff, citing national security and a reported jailbreak. Anthropic received the directive at 5:21pm ET and disabled both models for all customers the same evening to ensure compliance. The company published a transparent explanation, issued a customer apology ('We apologize for this disruption to our customers'), disclosed the government's stated reasoning and its own disagreement with the scope, and stated its intent to restore access when permitted. The benchmark scores the entity's conduct — recognizing, responding to, and reducing suffering — not regulatory exposure or sentiment about a government action. Anthropic's conduct pattern in this event is mildly positive on the Accountability dimension (ACC): same-day compliance with a costly directive, transparent public disclosure, explicit apology without legal deflection, and disclosure of the government's reasoning alongside Anthropic's own objection. The event does not lower any compassion-conduct dimension. The research composite lands at exactly 59.1, confirming the published Functional score. This is the first government-mandated suspension of a commercial AI model set documented by the benchmark across the AI-labs index. The methodological treatment — scoring the entity's conduct in the event, not the regulatory action itself — distinguishes this from a product-failure or safety-incident event. The Fable 5 / Mythos 5 suspension is a regulatory compliance event in which the entity's behavior was transparent and accountable.

evidence.quote: We apologize for this disruption to our customers
evidence.claim: Anthropic issued a direct customer apology on the day of the shutdown without legal deflection

evidence.quote: Anthropic disabled both models for all customers on June 12-13 to ensure compliance and published a transparent explanation
evidence.claim: Same-day compliance and public communication followed the Commerce Department directive

evidence.quote: US Commerce Department issued an export-control directive requiring Anthropic to suspend its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all foreign nationals
evidence.claim: The shutdown was government-mandated on national security grounds, not a product failure

topSignals.title: Bolivia 12.8 (Research: 8.7, Δ -4.1): Governance Breakdown Accumulates Below Threshold — Military Deployment Refusal Unresolved
topSignals.description: Bolivia's political and economic crisis deepened across the June 13 assessment window. Road blockades doubled to approximately 100 nationwide in the two weeks to June 3 (Euronews, June 3); the defence minister and education minister resigned June 3; shortages of food, medicine, and fuel were documented across La Paz, El Alto, and surrounding cities; and the government explicitly refused to rule out declaring a state of emergency or deploying the military against demonstrators. The government accused protesters of 'altering the democratic order.' The research composite of 8.7 is 4.1 points below the published 12.8 — sitting 0.9 points below the 5-point change-proposal threshold. This is Bolivia's second consecutive sub-threshold confirmed-deterioration reading in three days. The June 12 cycle carried an open change proposal (18.4 → 12.8, -5.6, within-Critical deepening). The June 13 assessment, using the June 12 applied score of 12.8 as its baseline, finds a further -4.1 research delta that does not yet clear the threshold but signals continued accumulation. The declared state-of-emergency trigger remains the clearest single forward event: if the government deploys the military and documents ensue, the research delta would likely cross 5 points and warrant a new proposal.

evidence.quote: about 100 road blockades... nearly double the number two weeks ago
evidence.claim: Bolivia's blockades doubled in two weeks to approximately 100 nationwide by June 3

evidence.quote: roadblocks have triggered shortages of food, medicine and fuel
evidence.claim: Blockades are cutting off essential supplies including medicine across major urban areas

evidence.quote: the government refused to rule out declaring a state of emergency and using the military
evidence.claim: Bolivia's government has not ruled out military deployment against demonstrators

topSignals.title: United States 17.5 (Research: 14.7, Δ -2.8): Medicaid Work-Requirement Rule and ICE Detention Deaths Document Ongoing Deterioration
topSignals.description: Two fresh in-window signals deepen the US Critical-band profile without generating a change proposal. First, HHS released the OBBBA Medicaid community-engagement interim final rule in early June 2026, implementing 80-hours-per-month work-reporting requirements effective late 2026/January 2027. CBO projections cited across multiple outlets (Center for American Progress, KFF, benefitsusa.org) estimate approximately 7.8 million people losing Medicaid coverage over the budget window — a foreseeable harm to low-income populations who face an administrative reporting burden unrelated to healthcare need. Second, ACLU (April 28, 2026) documents the 17th 2026 ICE detention death — a rate of approximately one death every six days — alongside a plan to convert 20+ warehouses to raise detention capacity to 96,600. KFF documents 46 detention deaths since January 2025. The US is already deep in the Critical band at 17.5, which limits composite movement: the research composite of 14.7 is 2.8 points below published, below the 5-point threshold. The Equity (5.0) and Accountability (5.0) dimensions are the weakest in the research assessment — reflecting the coverage-loss architecture and the under-acknowledgment of detention deaths. An unverified June 4 memo allegedly halting reporting of deaths of released detainees was treated as unconfirmed and scored conservatively.

evidence.quote: ~7.8M projected to lose Medicaid coverage
evidence.claim: CBO projects approximately 7.8 million people will lose Medicaid under the OBBBA community-engagement rule

evidence.quote: ACLU documents the 17th 2026 death in ICE detention, with deaths occurring roughly one every six days
evidence.claim: ICE detention deaths are accumulating at one every six days in 2026 with major capacity expansion underway

evidence.quote: 46 deaths since Jan 2025 as of Mar 18, 2026
evidence.claim: KFF documents 46 ICE detention deaths in the 15 months since January 2025

topSignals.title: Israel 0.0 (Floor Confirmed): OCHA Documents 936 Palestinians Killed Since Ceasefire, Meal Provision Down 55%
topSignals.description: OCHA's June 5/6, 2026 situation report documents 936 Palestinians killed and 2,903 injured since the October 10, 2025 ceasefire announcement; daily meal provision fell from approximately 1.5 million (mid-March) to approximately 678,000 (May 28), a 55% decline; the Zikim crossing was closed; and UNRWA operations are obstructed, with military facilities established at the Sheikh Jarrah compound. HRW published 'Gaza: Israel Curbs Aid, Kills Civilians During Ceasefire' on May 19, 2026. These harms are attributable to Israel's conduct toward the population of Gaza and reinforce the existing absolute floor at composite 0.0. The floor designation cannot move lower; the evidence is recorded to support the continued floor state. The OCHA and HRW documentation together constitute two independent Tier 2-3 sources covering killings, aid restriction, and infrastructure obstruction during a nominal ceasefire period — all three harm categories were already priced in the floor designation.

evidence.quote: 936 Palestinians killed and 2,903 injured since the Oct 10, 2025 ceasefire
evidence.claim: OCHA documents 936 fatalities and 2,903 injuries in Gaza in the period since the ceasefire announcement

evidence.quote: Gaza: Israel Curbs Aid, Kills Civilians During Ceasefire
evidence.claim: HRW published independent documentation of Israel's aid restriction and civilian killings during the ceasefire period

topSignals.title: Russia 0.0 (Floor Confirmed): OVD-Info Designated 'Extremist' June 4–5, Criminalizing Legal Aid for Rights Cases
topSignals.description: On June 4-5, 2026, Russia designated OVD-Info — the leading human-rights monitoring organization providing legal aid in freedom-of-assembly cases and operating a 24/7 abuse-reporting hotline — as an 'extremist organisation' on the Rosfinmonitoring list. HRW (June 5) and Meduza (June 4) independently confirmed the designation. The effect is to criminalize any cooperation with OVD-Info, including by lawyers, journalists, and activists who previously used its legal aid services. Amnesty (April 2026) separately documented young anti-war activists sentenced to 6-12 years. HRW World Report 2026 records approximately 1,299 war-opposition prosecutions with 373 still imprisoned. Russia is at the absolute floor (composite 0.0, all dimensions 1.0). The OVD-Info designation is documented as a new Signal Amplification suppression event (AWR/A4: independent monitors banned as extremist), reinforcing the floor without numerical movement.

evidence.quote: Russia designated OVD-Info an 'extremist organisation', criminalizing cooperation with the legal aid group
evidence.claim: Russia's June 4-5 extremist designation of OVD-Info eliminates a primary human rights monitoring channel

evidence.quote: OVD-Info provides legal aid in freedom-of-assembly cases and runs a 24/7 abuse-reporting hotline
evidence.claim: The ban closes a key legal protection and documentation channel for those detained at protests

topSignals.title: Sudan 0.0 (Floor Confirmed): 30M+ in Need, Appeal 16% Funded, 14M Displaced, GBV Need at 12.7M
topSignals.description: IOM Sudan Crisis Response Plan 2026 and UN reporting (April 2026) document the full scale of Sudan's humanitarian emergency entering its fourth year: 30 million people need humanitarian assistance; the $2.8 billion UN appeal is approximately 16% funded; approximately 14 million people are displaced; nearly two in five face emergency-level hunger; and 12.7 million people need gender-based violence support — a quadrupling since before the war. UN Women separately documents the GBV surge. Sudan is at the absolute floor (composite 0.0). The warring parties' conduct — mass displacement, siege conditions, and GBV at scale — constitutes the floor evidence base. The funding gap (84% of the appeal unfunded) compounds the humanitarian crisis that state parties have failed to address or acknowledge.

evidence.quote: 30M+ in need, appeal ~16% funded, 14M displaced
evidence.claim: Sudan's humanitarian emergency reaches 30 million in need with only 16% of the $2.8B UN appeal funded

evidence.quote: 12.7M need gender-based violence support — a quadrupling since before the war
evidence.claim: GBV need in Sudan has quadrupled since the conflict began, with 12.7 million people requiring support

topSignals.title: DRC 2.3 (Confirmed): Ebola PHEIC Reaches 676 Cases / 136 Deaths — Spreading Into New Health Zones
topSignals.description: As of June 12, 2026, the DRC Ebola outbreak (Bundibugyo strain) has reached 676 confirmed cases, 136 deaths, and 262 hospitalized. WHO declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) on May 17. Al Jazeera (June 12) reports the outbreak has spread into new health zones in North Kivu (2) and Ituri (1). CDC (June 5) confirms acceleration; there is no approved Bundibugyo-specific vaccine; MSF and WHO are responding with external coordination. The outbreak is expanding ahead of the July natural peak window with 18 health zones now affected. DRC's score holds at the published 2.3: the outbreak is an externally-led humanitarian emergency — the response capacity is minimal but the score's near-floor profile already reflects that constraint. The critical forward marker is whether state obstruction of international response teams is documented before July 31, which is the structural review deadline under the natural-disaster-overlay hold.

evidence.quote: 676 confirmed cases, 136 deaths, 262 hospitalized
evidence.claim: DRC's Ebola outbreak reached 676 cases and 136 deaths by June 12, 2026

evidence.quote: WHO described a 'catastrophic collision of disease and conflict'
evidence.claim: WHO's characterization frames the outbreak within the DRC's ongoing conflict context

topSignals.title: Sahel Cluster (Nigeria, Mali, Niger, Chad, Burkina Faso): Five Countries Confirm via Freshness Ruling
topSignals.description: Five Sahel countries — Nigeria (21.9), Mali (12.5), Niger (12.5), Chad (10.9), and Burkina Faso (6.3) — all confirm at published scores this cycle, but via a freshness ruling rather than positive in-window evidence. The FAO 52.8 million Sahel lean-season projection that flagged all five entities in the scanner was published January 27, 2026 — outside the 14-day assessment window — and was excluded as a score driver per the freshness rule. No fresh Nigeria-specific, Mali-specific, Niger-specific, Chad-specific, or Burkina Faso-specific governance-conduct evidence was found in the 14-day window. The published Critical scores for this cluster are correctly calibrated to absorb ongoing-crisis evidence; the freshness ruling is methodologically consistent with prior Sahel cycles. The June–August lean season remains a structural humanitarian stress period, and Phase 5 IPC famine designations for any individual country would constitute scorable evidence not currently in the window.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Second consecutive sub-threshold deterioration reading: research composite 8.7 vs. published 12.8, delta -4.1, sitting 0.9 points below the 5-point proposal threshold. Accountability dimension at 2.5 — the lowest of all eight dimensions.
boundaryWatch.note: Bolivia sits 4.1 research points below its published score in the second consecutive cycle with a sub-threshold deterioration reading. The governance breakdown pattern — doubled blockades, dual ministerial resignations, medicine and fuel shortages — is documented across two cycle windows. The triggering condition that would cross the 5-point threshold: a declared state of emergency, documented military deployment, or verified civilian casualties from a government response to protests.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A change proposal (20.6 → 14.7, Developing → Critical, Δ -5.9) carries forward from the June 12 cycle on the coercive severance architecture: sign-or-forfeit ultimatum, WARN pay absorbed as severance, and 'choosing the chips' leadership framing.
boundaryWatch.note: Oracle sits 0.6 points above the Critical boundary with an open change proposal from June 12 pending. The Washington state class action remains an active adjudication channel despite state WARN investigations closing without ruling. The June 12 proposal rests on the coercive-consent architecture as an independently scorable element; judicial resolution of the sign-or-forfeit theory is the primary forward trigger.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Change proposal from June 12 cycle: 86.1 → 79.0 (Δ -7.1, Exemplary → Established) on 7,000 layoffs announced during a period of increasing profits.
boundaryWatch.note: P&G's open June 12 proposal carries into this cycle. The Exemplary/Established boundary sits at approximately 80.0; the proposed 79.0 places P&G in Established. The two-year restructuring timeframe means ongoing quarterly disclosures are monitoring triggers: if scope expands or profit trajectory is confirmed above consensus while layoffs execute, the dimensional evidence deepens.

emergingRisks.risk: Bolivia continues accumulating sub-threshold deterioration evidence in consecutive cycles; a state-of-emergency declaration or documented military deployment would cross the 5-point proposal threshold.
emergingRisks.detail: Bolivia's research composite is 4.1 points below its published 12.8 — sitting 0.9 points below the change-proposal threshold. This is the second consecutive cycle with a sub-threshold deterioration reading. The tripling of this gap over the Bolivia arc (from the June 9 apply onward) means the next material event could produce a compounding within-Critical deepening. The Accountability dimension is at the near-floor (2.5); no correction or blame-acknowledgment has been documented.
emergingRisks.forwardDate: TBD — triggered by state-of-emergency declaration or military action

emergingRisks.risk: DRC Ebola (Bundibugyo) is entering the July natural peak window with 18 health zones affected and no approved vaccine; a single obstruction event documented before July 31 converts the natural-disaster overlay hold.
emergingRisks.detail: The outbreak has grown from 635/127 (June 11 cycle) to 676/136 (June 13 cycle) — 41 new cases and 9 deaths in two days. The July peak window for the Bundibugyo strain in Ituri is approaching. Contact tracing is sub-20% in most affected zones. No Bundibugyo-specific vaccine exists. The natural-disaster-overlay hold that has maintained DRC at 2.3 converts to a scorable downward event if documented state obstruction of international response teams is confirmed before July 31.

emergingRisks.risk: AI-model export controls establish a new regulatory axis for the AI-labs index; a second government-mandated suspension — of any lab — would establish pattern sufficiency for a new scoring category.
emergingRisks.detail: The June 12 Fable 5/Mythos 5 directive is the first government-mandated commercial AI model suspension documented across the AI-labs index. Anthropic's transparent-compliance conduct scored as net-neutral/mildly positive. A second suspension event — particularly one met with non-transparent compliance, delay, or selective disclosure — would generate the pattern density needed to formalize a new 'regulatory-compliance conduct' sub-category in the methodology. The EU AI Act August 2 GPAI deadline is the nearest parallel enforcement trigger.

emergingRisks.risk: US Medicaid coverage losses will begin materializing in late 2026/early 2027 as the OBBBA community-engagement rule takes effect; the scale of coverage loss will be scorable when verified enrollment data is published.
emergingRisks.detail: The OBBBA Medicaid community-engagement rule creates a projected 7.8M coverage-loss event in the late-2026/2027 implementation window. The US is currently at 17.5 (Critical) with a -2.8 research delta this cycle. When enrollment data begins to reflect actual coverage loss, the documented harm will be scorable as a new-evidence-of-realized-harm event rather than a projected-harm accumulation. A third consecutive sub-threshold reading on the US (alongside the confirmed post-apply priced-continuation profile) would also trigger a methodology review of whether the published 17.5 baseline has aged below its calibrated level.

emergingRisks.risk: Iran's political execution rate (31+ since March 19, five imminent as of June 9) is approaching the frequency threshold that would trigger a review of whether a second dominant harm category has emerged alongside the existing single-dominant-extremity designation.
emergingRisks.detail: Iran's published 2.5 reflects a single-dominant-extremity hold on the mass political execution pattern. The research composite of 1.3 (delta -1.2) this cycle is consistent with prior near-floor readings. The conversion trigger is not frequency alone — it is a documented second dominant harm category at scale (mass detention of a distinct minority, forced disappearances at scale, or a new systematic harm category). The NCRI June 9 report of five imminent executions across Sheiban and Ahvaz Prisons suggests the pattern is not decelerating.
emergingRisks.forwardDate: TBD — contingent on second dominant harm category documentation

sectorAlerts.headline: First Government-Mandated AI Model Shutdown Establishes Regulatory-Compliance Conduct Category
observations: The June 12 Commerce Department directive against Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models is the first government-mandated commercial AI model suspension in the benchmark's AI-labs index history.

Anthropic's conduct — same-day compliance, transparent public disclosure, customer apology, and disclosure of the government's reasoning alongside disagreement — is the first documented example of a lab's regulatory-compliance conduct being evaluated as a standalone scoring event.

The methodological distinction (scoring conduct in the event, not the regulatory action itself) has implications for any future AI-lab suspension: non-transparent compliance, delay, or selective disclosure would be scored negatively.

EU AI Act August 2 GPAI deadline remains the next hard enforcement trigger; no assessed lab has published required documentation. The Anthropic event suggests a pattern of regulatory engagement that will intersect with EU compliance posture.

sectorAlerts.sector: Countries — Active Perpetration and Floor Cohort
sectorAlerts.headline: Three Absolute Floors Reinforced with Fresh UN, HRW, and IOM Evidence in a Single Cycle
observations: Israel (0.0), Russia (0.0), and Sudan (0.0) all absorb new evidence in this cycle that deepens but cannot numerically change the absolute-floor designation.

The three floor reinforcements share a structural feature: in all three cases, civil-society monitoring capacity is being actively suppressed — UNRWA obstructed in Gaza, OVD-Info designated extremist in Russia, and humanitarian access to Sudan at only 16% funding.

The simultaneous monitoring-suppression pattern across three floor entities is analytically significant: it suggests that at the absolute-floor level, one of the consistent state conduct features is limitation of the international documentation capacity that the benchmark's scoring methodology relies on.

Palestine (25.0, Developing) confirms with a critical attribution ruling: the OCHA harm evidence is attributable to Israel's conduct, not the Palestinian Authority, and scores to Israel's assessment, not Palestine's. The PA continues operating under blockade-imposed constraints.

sectorAlerts.headline: Bolivia's Sub-Threshold Deterioration Is the Fastest Accumulation Pattern in the Current Critical-Band Cohort
observations: Bolivia's research composite of 8.7 is 4.1 points below its published 12.8 in only the second cycle since its June 12 within-Critical deepening proposal. The accumulation rate is faster than any other entity in the Critical band in the current 30-day window.

The US (research -2.8), Bolivia (-4.1), and Iran (-1.2) all confirm sub-threshold this cycle — forming a three-entity sub-threshold deterioration cluster across the Critical band with different underlying drivers.

DRC's Ebola trajectory (676/136 as of June 12, up from 635/127 two days prior) is the most kinetically uncertain variable in the Critical-band cohort. The July peak window is the structural risk horizon.

The Sahel cluster (Nigeria, Mali, Niger, Chad, Burkina Faso) demonstrates the freshness rule operating as intended: five entities with ongoing crisis evidence not generating proposals because the in-window evidence was published outside the 14-day window.

sectorAlerts.headline: Three Open Change Proposals Carry Forward; Rotation Confirms Five Entities at Baseline
observations: P&G (86.1 → 79.0 proposed, June 12) and Oracle (20.6 → 14.7 proposed, June 12) carry forward as open proposals. Bolivia's June 12 proposal (12.8 baseline) also carries forward.

Five Fortune 500 rotation entities (Benchmark Electronics, Vistra, Toll Brothers, AvalonBay, Group 1 Automotive) all confirm at the uniform 25.0 baseline with no material compassion-relevant evidence in the window. This is consistent with the expected evidence distribution for Fortune 500 entities not currently in a newsworthy conduct event.

The Vistra finding is methodologically clean: three separate out-of-window items (2019 settlement, 2022 WARN notices, January 2025 Moss Landing fire suit) were identified and correctly excluded by the freshness rule.

The baseline-confirmation rate for Fortune 500 rotation entities continues to be high; the signal-to-noise ratio for non-newsworthy Fortune 500 entities is low, consistent with calibration expectations.

methodologyNotes.name: GOVERNMENT-MANDATED-AI-SHUTDOWN-CONDUCT-SCORING
methodologyNotes.description: First application of conduct scoring for a government-mandated AI model suspension. The event is scored on the entity's behavior (compliance posture, transparency, customer communication, disclosure of government reasoning) rather than on the regulatory action itself or market/reputational effects. Transparent same-day compliance is net-neutral to mildly positive on the Accountability dimension. Future applications: non-transparent compliance, delay, or selective disclosure would score negatively on ACC and INT dimensions.

methodologyNotes.name: FRESHNESS-RULE-REGIONAL-PROJECTION-EXCLUSION
methodologyNotes.description: A regional humanitarian projection published outside the 14-day assessment window does not drive individual country proposals even when it is the primary scanner trigger. The FAO 52.8M Sahel lean-season projection (published January 27, 2026) affected five countries in the scanner but was excluded for all five in the assessor cycle. Country-specific fresh-window evidence is required for each entity's assessment. First large-group application of this rule (five entities simultaneously).

methodologyNotes.name: ATTRIBUTION-VICTIM-ENTITY-SCORING
methodologyNotes.description: Harms documented against a population are scored to the entity whose conduct caused the harm, not to the population's governing entity. OCHA documentation of 936 Palestinian deaths and 55% meal-provision decline is attributed to Israel's conduct and scores to Israel's assessment. Palestine, as the governing entity for the affected population operating under externally imposed blockade constraints, scores on its own conduct given its constrained capacity.

forwardTriggers.trigger: July natural peak window for Bundibugyo Ebola strain in Ituri — 18 health zones affected, no approved vaccine, 676/136 as of June 12. Documented state obstruction of international response before this date converts the natural-disaster-overlay hold to a scored downward event.

EU AI Act GPAI compliance deadline — first hard transparency documentation requirement for large AI models. No assessed AI lab has published required documentation. Compliance posture at the deadline will generate scoring events across the AI-labs index.

State-of-emergency declaration or documented military deployment against protesters. The research delta is 4.1 points (0.9 below the 5-point threshold). Either event would likely cross the threshold and generate a new within-Critical deepening proposal.

Washington state class-action ruling on the sign-or-forfeit severance structure. The June 12 change proposal (20.6 → 14.7, Developing → Critical) carries forward; adverse adjudication strengthens the coercive-consent foundation of the proposal.

OBBBA Medicaid community-engagement rule implementation — late 2026/January 2027. Coverage-loss materialization generates scorable evidence when verified enrollment data is published. CBO projects approximately 7.8M coverage losses.

Documentation of a second dominant harm category (mass detention of a distinct minority at scale, forced disappearances at scale) would break the single-dominant-extremity hold and trigger a reassessment toward the absolute floor.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: If Anthropic's transparent export-control compliance confirms its 59.1 score, does the ACC dimension adequately distinguish between entities that disclose government directives publicly and those that comply without disclosure — and would the Fable 5/Mythos 5 conduct pattern hold if a future directive prohibited disclosure itself?
dailyOpeningQuestion.tensionFraming: Transparency in compliance versus compelled silence: does the Accountability dimension resolve this distinction?
